sources.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (answer key for odd-numbered review questions)

meta.notes: Psychology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch. 2 review-questions page; correct answers taken from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), which publishes answers to odd-numbered review questions only (so only odd-numbered items are included). The live OpenStax page shows a CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license plus a no-LLM-training notice; per the SpeedyCAT PRD this content is used human-facing and non-AI, mirroring psbb-001 in questions.json. Q7 omitted: the Solutions Guide left its answer blank.

hints.prompt: The stem pairs the blank with 'falsifiable.' What does it mean for a scientific hypothesis to be falsifiable?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It can be contradicted by empirical evidence if it happens to be wrong

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has already been proven true for every possible case

choices.label: C
choices.text: It cannot be tested by observation in any way

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is based purely on the researcher's personal opinion

hints.rationale: A falsifiable hypothesis is one that empirical evidence could potentially disprove.

hints.prompt: Why do scientists say a strongly supported hypothesis is 'supported' rather than 'proven'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence can strengthen a hypothesis but can never establish it with absolute certainty

choices.label: B
choices.text: Scientists are not permitted to gather any supporting evidence

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypotheses are mere opinions and so cannot be examined at all

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Proven' and 'falsifiable' are simply two words for the same idea

hints.rationale: Data can support a hypothesis but never yield absolute proof, so 'proven' overstates the case.

hints.prompt: A classmate wants to fill the blank with 'provable.' Which point best shows this is a trap option to rule out?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Empirical evidence supports rather than definitively proves a hypothesis, so 'provable' overstates science

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Provable' means exactly the same thing as 'falsifiable'

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hypotheses can never be observed or measured

choices.label: D
choices.text: Science delivers absolute certainty, so 'provable' is the ideal word

hints.rationale: 'Provable' overstates science and can be eliminated, since hypotheses are supported, not proven.

choices.label: A
choices.text: observable

choices.label: B
choices.text: original

choices.label: C
choices.text: provable

choices.label: D
choices.text: testable

questions.explanation: A scientific hypothesis must be both testable and falsifiable, meaning it can be supported or refuted by empirical evidence. 'Provable' overstates the case, because hypotheses are supported rather than definitively proven.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 2 Q1 = D.

hints.prompt: On what basis does science mainly establish its knowledge claims?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Systematic, objective observation and measurement of the world

choices.label: B
choices.text: The strength of the researcher's personal convictions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Long-standing tradition and the authority of experts

choices.label: D
choices.text: Whichever conclusion happens to be most popular

hints.rationale: Science grounds its knowledge in objective, systematic observation and measurement.

hints.prompt: How does a scientific way of knowing differ from an intuitive or subjective one?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It relies on measurable, sharable evidence rather than private hunches or personal opinion

choices.label: B
choices.text: It relies on gut feelings, exactly as intuition does

choices.label: C
choices.text: It treats personal opinion as the strongest form of proof

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is no meaningful difference between them

hints.rationale: Scientific knowing depends on objective evidence, unlike intuition (hunches) or subjectivity (opinion).

hints.prompt: One option describes scientific knowledge as 'permanent.' Why can that choice be ruled out?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Scientific conclusions are revised as new evidence accumulates, so they are not permanent

choices.label: B
choices.text: Scientific knowledge, once published, can never be changed

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Permanent' is simply another word for 'empirical'

choices.label: D
choices.text: Scientists never collect any new data after a study ends

hints.rationale: Science is self-correcting and updates with new evidence, so 'permanent' is a distractor to eliminate.

choices.label: A
choices.text: intuitive

choices.label: B
choices.text: empirical

choices.label: C
choices.text: permanent

choices.label: D
choices.text: subjective

questions.explanation: Scientific knowledge is empirical: it is grounded in objective, systematic observation and measurement rather than in intuition or subjective opinion.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 2 Q3 = B.

hints.prompt: Freud studied a few clients in great depth over a long time. Research methods that emphasize studying very few people in depth (rather than many people briefly) prioritize which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Depth of information about a small number of individuals

choices.label: B
choices.text: Breadth of shallow data across a very large sample

choices.label: C
choices.text: Numerical data drawn only from public records

choices.label: D
choices.text: Avoiding any direct contact with the people studied

hints.rationale: The scenario emphasizes deep information about a few individuals rather than broad, shallow data.

hints.prompt: How does a survey differ from the in-depth interview approach Freud used?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A survey gathers relatively brief, standardized responses from many respondents, not deep data from a few

choices.label: B
choices.text: A survey studies a single person intensively for many years

choices.label: C
choices.text: A survey requires secretly observing people in public settings

choices.label: D
choices.text: A survey relies only on historical documents

hints.rationale: Surveys sample many people briefly, unlike the deep study of a few individuals described here.

hints.prompt: Freud actively interviewed living clients. Which method can be ruled out because it does NOT involve interacting with participants at all?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Archival research, which analyzes pre-existing records

choices.label: B
choices.text: Interviewing clients across many sessions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Asking clients detailed follow-up questions

choices.label: D
choices.text: Talking with patients about their lives over time

hints.rationale: Archival research uses existing records and requires no participant interaction, so it can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Sigmund Freud developed his theory of human personality by conducting in-depth interviews over an extended period of time with a few clients. This type of research approach is known as a(n): ________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: archival research

choices.label: B
choices.text: case study

choices.label: C
choices.text: naturalistic observation

choices.label: D
choices.text: survey

questions.explanation: Studying a small number of individuals in great depth over an extended period is a case study. Freud built his personality theory from such intensive study of a few clients.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 2 Q5 = B.

hints.prompt: The stem's key phrase is 'using existing records.' Relying on information gathered before a study began (rather than collected firsthand for it) means using what kind of data?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Secondary data (records or datasets created earlier, often by others)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Primary data gathered directly by the researcher for this study

choices.label: C
choices.text: Data that do not yet exist and must be invented

choices.label: D
choices.text: Data that can come only from a laboratory experiment

hints.rationale: Existing records are secondary data: information collected before, and often independently of, the current study.

hints.prompt: How does using existing records differ from naturalistic observation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Existing records were compiled previously, whereas naturalistic observation collects new firsthand data by watching behavior as it happens

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both approaches require the researcher to watch behavior live

choices.label: C
choices.text: Existing records can be obtained only by surveying strangers

choices.label: D
choices.text: Naturalistic observation uses nothing but historical documents

hints.rationale: Naturalistic observation gathers new firsthand data, unlike the reuse of pre-existing records.

hints.prompt: Which method can be ruled out because it is defined by studying the SAME participants repeatedly over a long period, not by using existing records?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Longitudinal research

choices.label: B
choices.text: Analyzing old hospital files

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pulling figures from a historical census

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reviewing archived newspaper articles

hints.rationale: Longitudinal research is defined by repeated measurement over time, so it can be eliminated as the 'existing records' method.

questions.stem: Using existing records to try to answer a research question is known as ________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: naturalistic observation

choices.label: B
choices.text: survey research

choices.label: C
choices.text: longitudinal research

choices.label: D
choices.text: archival research

questions.explanation: Archival research uses pre-existing records or data sets (such as historical documents or databases) to answer a research question.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 2 Q9 = D.

hints.prompt: A tool made of a standardized list of questions is used to gather what kind of data directly from many respondents?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Self-report data (respondents' own answers about themselves)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Observational data recorded by hidden researchers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Physiological data from brain scans

choices.label: D
choices.text: Archival data taken from old records

hints.rationale: A list of questions gathers self-report data: respondents' own answers.

hints.prompt: How does a questionnaire-based method differ from a case study?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It collects standardized answers from many people, whereas a case study examines one or a few individuals in depth

choices.label: B
choices.text: It examines a single person intensively for years, just like a case study

choices.label: C
choices.text: It requires secretly watching people in public

choices.label: D
choices.text: It relies solely on pre-existing archives

hints.rationale: Questionnaire methods sample many respondents briefly, unlike the in-depth focus of a case study.

hints.prompt: The stem describes a researcher-written list of questions given to respondents. Which option can be ruled out because it does NOT involve asking respondents any questions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Naturalistic observation, which records behavior without questioning anyone

choices.label: B
choices.text: Handing out a printed questionnaire

choices.label: C
choices.text: Emailing a set of written questions

choices.label: D
choices.text: Asking respondents to answer items on a form

hints.rationale: Naturalistic observation involves watching, not asking questions, so it can be eliminated.

questions.stem: A(n) ________ is a list of questions developed by a researcher that can be administered in paper form.

choices.label: A
choices.text: archive

choices.label: B
choices.text: case Study

choices.label: C
choices.text: naturalistic observation

choices.label: D
choices.text: survey

questions.explanation: A survey is a list of questions, administered on paper or electronically, used to gather self-report data from many respondents.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax (option B casing 'case Study' as printed). correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 2 Q11 = D.

hints.prompt: What does a correlation between two variables tell you?

choices.label: A
choices.text: That the two variables tend to change together in a predictable pattern

choices.label: B
choices.text: That one variable definitely causes the other

choices.label: C
choices.text: That the two variables are entirely unrelated

choices.label: D
choices.text: That the variables were manipulated in an experiment

hints.rationale: A correlation shows the variables vary together, without proving that one causes the other.

hints.prompt: What distinguishes a POSITIVE correlation from a NEGATIVE one?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In a positive correlation the variables move in the same direction; in a negative correlation they move in opposite directions

choices.label: B
choices.text: In a positive correlation the variables move in opposite directions

choices.label: C
choices.text: A positive correlation proves causation, while a negative one does not

choices.label: D
choices.text: There is no difference between the two

hints.rationale: Positive correlation means same direction; negative correlation means opposite directions.

hints.prompt: Height and weight are said to be POSITIVELY correlated (they move in the same direction). Which statement correctly identifies options that can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Both 'taller people are usually thinner' and 'shorter people are usually heavier' can be eliminated, since they describe an opposite-direction relationship

choices.label: B
choices.text: The option stating there is no relationship must be correct

choices.label: C
choices.text: Any option describing a same-direction relationship should be eliminated

choices.label: D
choices.text: None of the options can be eliminated

hints.rationale: Both inverse-relationship options contradict a positive correlation and can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Height and weight are positively correlated. This means that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: There is no relationship between height and weight.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Usually, the taller someone is, the thinner they are.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Usually, the shorter someone is, the heavier they are.

choices.label: D
choices.text: As height increases, typically weight increases.

questions.explanation: A positive correlation means two variables move in the same direction: as one increases, the other typically increases too. So taller people tend to weigh more.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 2 Q13 = D.

hints.prompt: What does a NEGATIVE correlation between two variables describe?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As one variable increases, the other tends to decrease

choices.label: B
choices.text: As one variable increases, the other also tends to increase

choices.label: C
choices.text: One variable directly causes a change in the other

choices.label: D
choices.text: The two variables have no relationship at all

hints.rationale: A negative correlation means that as one variable rises, the other tends to fall.

hints.prompt: This item asks for a CORRELATION. Why must the correct statement avoid claiming that one variable CAUSES the other?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A correlation shows that two variables are related but cannot by itself establish that one causes the other

choices.label: B
choices.text: A correlation always proves what causes the relationship

choices.label: C
choices.text: Correlation and causation are identical concepts

choices.label: D
choices.text: Correlations can be measured only inside experiments

hints.rationale: Correlation indicates association only; it cannot establish causation.

hints.prompt: Two options use explicit cause-and-effect wording: that TV 'leads to' poor performance and that TV 'interferes with' preparation. What is the best reason to eliminate BOTH?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They assert a causal effect, which correlational data alone cannot support

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are the only options that mention television

choices.label: C
choices.text: They describe a positive rather than a negative correlation

choices.label: D
choices.text: They fail to mention exam grades

hints.rationale: Both statements claim causation, which a correlation cannot justify, so both can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Which statement best illustrates a negative correlation between the number of hours spent watching TV the week before an exam and the grade on that exam?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Watching too much television leads to poor exam performance.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Smart students watch less television.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Viewing television interferes with a student's ability to prepare for the upcoming exam.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Students who watch more television perform more poorly on their exams.

questions.explanation: A correlation describes a relationship without claiming causation. Only choice D simply states the inverse relationship; the other options imply that TV viewing causes poor performance, which a correlation cannot establish.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 2 Q15 = D.

hints.prompt: The stem describes giving everyone in the POPULATION an equal chance of being chosen for a study. Doing this mainly serves what goal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Obtaining a representative sample so results can be generalized to the population

choices.label: B
choices.text: Guaranteeing that a treatment causes an observed effect

choices.label: C
choices.text: Defining a variable in precise, measurable terms

choices.label: D
choices.text: Providing a fake treatment to a control group

hints.rationale: Giving everyone an equal chance of selection yields a representative sample and supports generalizability.

hints.prompt: What is the difference between random SELECTION from a population and random ASSIGNMENT to groups?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Random selection decides WHO enters the study; random assignment decides which GROUP a chosen participant is placed in

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are two names for exactly the same procedure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Random selection sorts participants into experimental groups

choices.label: D
choices.text: Random assignment decides who is invited from the population

hints.rationale: Selection picks who enters the study; assignment sorts already-chosen participants into groups.

hints.prompt: The stem is about HOW participants are chosen. Which two options can be eliminated because they have nothing to do with selecting participants?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Operationalizing' (defining variables) and the 'placebo effect' (a response to a fake treatment)

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Random assignment' and 'random sampling'

choices.label: C
choices.text: The 'placebo effect' and 'random assignment'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Operationalizing' and 'random sampling'

hints.rationale: Operationalizing and the placebo effect are unrelated to participant selection, so both can be eliminated.

questions.stem: ________ means that everyone in the population has the same likelihood of being asked to participate in the study.

choices.label: A
choices.text: operationalizing

choices.label: B
choices.text: placebo effect

choices.label: C
choices.text: random assignment

choices.label: D
choices.text: random sampling

questions.explanation: Random sampling means every member of the population has an equal chance of being selected to participate, which supports generalizability. Random assignment, by contrast, sorts already-selected participants into groups.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 2 Q17 = D.

hints.prompt: The stem is about giving a concept a precise, measurable definition so others know exactly how it was measured. This process is best captured by which idea?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Specifying the exact operations or measurements used to represent a concept

choices.label: B
choices.text: Randomly placing participants into different groups

choices.label: C
choices.text: Extending a study's findings to a broader population

choices.label: D
choices.text: Choosing participants by chance from the population

hints.rationale: Defining a concept by the concrete operations used to measure it is what the stem describes.

hints.prompt: How does defining a concept in measurable terms differ from 'generalizing'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Defining in measurable terms specifies HOW a variable is measured, whereas generalizing extends results to a broader population

choices.label: B
choices.text: They mean exactly the same thing

choices.label: C
choices.text: Defining in measurable terms means applying results to everyone

choices.label: D
choices.text: Generalizing means writing a precise measurement procedure

hints.rationale: Operational definitions specify measurement; generalizing extends findings. They are two different ideas.

hints.prompt: The stem is about DEFINING concepts clearly, not about handling participants. Which two options can be eliminated because they concern participants rather than definitions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Randomly assign' and 'randomly select'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Operationalize' and 'generalize'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Randomly select' and 'generalize'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Randomly assign' and 'operationalize'

hints.rationale: Random assignment and random selection deal with participants, not with defining concepts, so both can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Researchers must ________ important concepts in their studies so others would have a clear understanding of exactly how those concepts were defined.

choices.label: A
choices.text: randomly assign

choices.label: B
choices.text: randomly select

choices.label: C
choices.text: operationalize

choices.label: D
choices.text: generalize

questions.explanation: To operationalize a concept is to define it in terms of specific, measurable operations, so others know exactly how each variable was measured.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 2 Q19 = C.

hints.prompt: Research ethics oversight often involves a review committee such as an IRB or IACUC. What is the main role of such a committee?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To review and approve the ethical treatment of research subjects before a study proceeds

choices.label: B
choices.text: To guarantee that a study's hypothesis turns out to be correct

choices.label: C
choices.text: To collect all of the study's data for the researcher

choices.label: D
choices.text: To publish the study's results in a journal

hints.rationale: Review committees evaluate the ethics of proposed research before it is carried out.

hints.prompt: Terms such as 'informed consent,' 'deception,' and 'debriefing' refer to what, as opposed to an IRB or IACUC?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ethical procedures or practices used within a study, not the committees that review studies

choices.label: B
choices.text: The formal names of research oversight committees

choices.label: C
choices.text: Types of statistical analysis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Methods of randomly sampling participants

hints.rationale: Informed consent, deception, and debriefing are procedures within research, whereas IRB and IACUC are oversight committees.

hints.prompt: The stem contrasts the oversight COMMITTEE for animal research with the one for human research. Which choices can be eliminated because they pair ethical PROCEDURES rather than committees?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The 'informed consent; deception' and 'deception; debriefing' options

choices.label: B
choices.text: The 'IACUC; IRB' and 'IRB; IACUC' options

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only the 'IACUC; IRB' option

choices.label: D
choices.text: None of the options can be eliminated

hints.rationale: Two options list ethical procedures instead of oversight committees, so they can be eliminated.

questions.stem: ________ is to animal research as ________ is to human research.

choices.label: A
choices.text: informed consent; deception

choices.label: B
choices.text: IACUC; IRB

choices.label: C
choices.text: IRB; IACUC

choices.label: D
choices.text: deception; debriefing

questions.explanation: An IACUC (Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee) reviews animal research, while an IRB (Institutional Review Board) reviews research involving human participants.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 2 Q21 = B.

hints.prompt: A core ethical principle of research with humans concerns each person's freedom to decide whether to take part. This principle protects participants' what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autonomy: their right to freely decide whether to participate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Their odds of winning a cash prize

choices.label: C
choices.text: Their fame or public recognition

choices.label: D
choices.text: Their random placement into study groups

hints.rationale: Ethical research protects participants' autonomy: their free choice about taking part.

hints.prompt: Why is simply REWARDING people to take part not enough to make participation ethical?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Large rewards can pressure or coerce people, undermining a genuinely free choice

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rewards of any kind are illegal in research

choices.label: C
choices.text: Offering rewards automatically makes a study more scientific

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ethics requires that every participant be paid

hints.rationale: Excessive incentives can be coercive, so being 'rewarded' does not satisfy the requirement for free, uncoerced participation.

hints.prompt: The stem is about the ETHICS of taking part, not study logistics. Which two options can be eliminated because they describe methodology or exposure rather than an ethical requirement of participation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Random' (a sampling or assignment procedure) and 'public' (exposure of participants)

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Voluntary' and 'rewarded'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Rewarded' and 'random'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Public' and 'voluntary'

hints.rationale: 'Random' concerns methodology and 'public' concerns exposure, so neither is the ethical requirement described; both can be eliminated.

questions.stem: A person's participation in a research project must be ________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: random

choices.label: B
choices.text: rewarded

choices.label: C
choices.text: voluntary

choices.label: D
choices.text: public

questions.explanation: Ethical research requires that participation be voluntary: people must freely choose to take part (and may withdraw at any time) without coercion.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 2 Q23 = C.
